items.title: Chrome V8 Zero-Day CVE-2026-11645 (CVSS 8.8) Actively Exploited — 5th Chrome 0-Day of 2026
items.summary: Out-of-bounds memory access in Chrome's V8 JavaScript/WebAssembly engine allows a remote attacker to execute arbitrary code via a crafted HTML page. Google patched it in Chrome 149.0.7827.102/103 on June 8, 2026 — the fifth Chrome zero-day exploited in the wild this year. CISA added it to the KEV catalog and a $55,000 bug bounty was awarded to the reporting researcher.
items.why: Actively exploited in the wild with no public details on threat actors to prevent further abuse. Affects all Chromium-based browsers. Immediate update to Chrome 149+ is critical for all users and enterprises.

items.title: Check Point VPN Auth Bypass CVE-2026-50751 (CVSS 9.3) Exploited by Qilin Ransomware Affiliate
items.summary: Critical authentication bypass in Check Point Security Gateway's deprecated IKEv1 VPN protocol allows attackers to establish VPN sessions without valid credentials by exploiting a logic flaw in certificate validation. Exploitation observed since May 7, 2026 with escalation in early June. Check Point confirmed a Qilin ransomware affiliate used this zero-day to breach dozens of organizations globally before a hotfix was released.
items.why: CVSS 9.3 with confirmed ransomware exploitation. Any org running Check Point VPN with IKEv1 enabled is at immediate risk of credential-free network access. Emergency hotfix is available — apply without waiting for a regular patch cycle.

items.title: APT28 'Operation Neusploit': Weaponizes MS Office CVE-2026-21509 Within 24 Hours to Hit European Military and Maritime Targets
items.summary: Russian state-sponsored APT28 (Fancy Bear / FROZENLAKE) exploited Microsoft Office OLE security bypass CVE-2026-21509 within 24 hours of its public disclosure, launching spear-phishing campaigns against maritime and transport organizations across Poland, Slovenia, Turkey, Greece, UAE, and Ukraine. The multi-stage infection chain deploys a novel Outlook VBA backdoor (NotDoor) and a modified Covenant C2 implant, with all C2 traffic routed through legitimate cloud infrastructure.
items.why: Demonstrates adversary 1-day weaponization speed at nation-state scale — 24 hours from patch release to live exploitation. Novel cloud-hosted C2 and Outlook backdoor represent new evasion tradecraft targeting NATO-adjacent infrastructure.
items.source: Trellix / The Hacker News / Zscaler ThreatLabz

items.title: Iranian APT Screening Serpens Deploys MiniUpdate and MiniJunk V2 RATs via Azure C2 in Conflict-Driven Espionage Surge
items.summary: Palo Alto Unit 42 tracked Iranian threat group Screening Serpens deploying six new RAT variants across two malware families — newly discovered MiniUpdate and evolved MiniJunk V2 — against targets in the US, Israel, UAE, and additional Middle East entities. Activity surged after the February 28 regional conflict outbreak. Each target gets a unique set of 3-5 Azure-hosted C2 domains. The group evades EDR by exploiting a legitimate .NET runtime configuration file to disable security monitoring entirely, without memory patching.
items.why: Conflict-correlated espionage surge with a novel EDR evasion technique that abuses the .NET runtime's own config mechanism — no shellcode or memory patching required. Highly targeted C2 infrastructure per victim makes detection and attribution harder.

items.title: Mandiant M-Trends 2026: PROMPTFLUX and PROMPTSTEAL Are First Confirmed Malware to Query Live LLMs During Execution
items.summary: Mandiant's M-Trends 2026 report documents two malware families using live LLM API calls mid-execution: PROMPTFLUX (a VBScript dropper) uses the Gemini API to rewrite its own source code hourly, generating polymorphic variants that evade signature detection on demand. PROMPTSTEAL, attributed to APT28, queries an LLM to dynamically generate Windows shell commands for document theft against Ukrainian targets — the first confirmed state-sponsored malware with live LLM integration. Report also notes median dwell time rose to 14 days as attackers focus on destroying backups.
items.why: Marks a qualitative leap in malware sophistication: runtime AI integration enables goal-driven, polymorphic behavior that defeats signature-based and behavior-static detection. Defenders must shift to behavioral AI detectors and LLM API egress monitoring.
